perplexity.answer:
## Executive takeaway

Jordan’s most consequential recent development is the advance of the **Aqaba–Amman National Water Carrier**, a desalination, transmission and renewable-energy project designed to supply **300 million cubic metres of drinking water annually**. Politically, Amman is taking a firm position on Iranian attacks and proxy activity while expanding diplomatic and economic relations beyond the region. **None of the retrieved reporting identifies a new UAE-specific investment, trade, AI, energy or humanitarian agreement with Jordan**, although Jordan’s stance on Gulf security is directly relevant to the UAE.

One live-search request timed out and several searches returned no results, so UAE-specific coverage may be incomplete. The summary below relies on the successfully retrieved reporting and does not infer unreported UAE involvement.

---

## 1. Political and diplomatic developments

### Jordan hardens its position on Iran and regional security

Jordanian Foreign Minister and Deputy Prime Minister **Ayman Safadi** reportedly stressed that any agreement with Iran should be comprehensive, end Tehran’s support for proxy groups and confront its wider destabilising activities.

Separately, Jordan’s Foreign Ministry condemned reported Iranian attacks against **Bahrain, Qatar and Kuwait**, describing them as “heinous.”

**Relevance to the UAE:** This is the clearest UAE-related development in the material. Although the UAE was not named in the reported condemnation, Jordan is publicly aligning itself with the security of Gulf states and opposing attacks on GCC countries and Iranian proxy activity.

Sources:

- [Safadi’s position on a comprehensive Iran agreement](https://www.facebook.com/JewishInsider/posts/jordanian-deputy-prime-minister-ayman-safadi-repeatedly-emphasized-on-wednesday-/1649421920517133/)
- [Jordan condemns attacks targeting Bahrain, Qatar and Kuwait](https://www.facebook.com/RoyaNewsEnglish/posts/jordans-ministry-of-foreign-affairs-condemned-the-iranian-attacks-targeting-bahr/1489926019842100/)

![Jordan regional-security report](https://lookaside.fbsbx.com/lookaside/crawler/media/?media_id=1489925546508814)

### Safadi engages Iraq in Washington

Safadi also met Iraqi Prime Minister **Ali Faleh Al-Zaidi** in Washington, D.C. The retrieved excerpt confirms the meeting but does not provide enough detail to establish specific agreements or outcomes.

Source:

- [Iraqi Prime Minister’s meeting with Safadi](https://www.facebook.com/IraqiPMO.Eng/posts/prime-minister-ali-faleh-al-zaidi-receives-jordanian-foreign-minister-in-washing/1020820724177576/)

### Jordan and Venezuela pursue an economic-cooperation roadmap

During a working visit to Caracas, Safadi met Venezuelan Acting President **Delcy Rodríguez** and Foreign Minister **Yvan Gil**. The parties agreed to develop a roadmap covering:

- Energy, oil and gas
- Tourism and culture
- Trade and investment
- Political consultations
- Steps to accelerate bilateral agreements

This indicates Jordan’s effort to diversify economic and diplomatic relationships, including in hydrocarbons and investment.

Source:

- [Jordan and Venezuela agree on an economic-cooperation roadmap](https://www.middleeastmonitor.com/20260221-jordan-venezuela-agree-on-roadmap-for-economic-cooperation/)

---

## 2. Economic and development developments

### Germany commits a €684 million development package

Jordan and Germany signed the official minutes of bilateral development-cooperation talks, allocating **€684 million** to priority projects aligned with Jordan’s:

- Economic-reform agenda
- Public-sector reform agenda
- Broader development priorities

The talks were led by Jordanian Planning and International Cooperation Minister **Zeina Toukan** and Germany’s Federal Ministry for Economic Cooperation and Development.

**UAE relevance:** No UAE participation is reported, but the package demonstrates the scale of external financing being mobilised for Jordan’s reform and development programme.

Source:

- [Jordan–Germany €684 million development package](https://www.facebook.com/thejordantimes/posts/jordan-and-germany-have-signed-the-official-minutes-of-their-bilateral-developme/1434803542027835/)

![Jordan–Germany development cooperation](https://lookaside.fbsbx.com/lookaside/crawler/media/?media_id=1434803488694507)

---

## 3. Water, food security and major infrastructure

### National Water Carrier moves toward construction

The latest retrieved project update, dated **5 July 2026**, says construction of the Aqaba–Amman National Water Carrier is expected to begin in the **fourth quarter of 2026**. Construction should take approximately four years, with water pumping scheduled for the **fourth quarter of 2030**.

The project comprises:

- A Red Sea desalination plant near Aqaba
- Approximately **450 kilometres** of water-transmission infrastructure
- A solar-power plant
- Annual production of **300 million cubic metres**
- Expected supply equivalent to approximately **40% of Jordan’s drinking-water needs**

The report says final agreements required for financial close were still being prepared.

Source:

- [National Water Carrier construction expected to begin in late 2026](https://jordantimes.com/news/local/national-water-carrier-project-construction-to-begin-in-late-2026-official)

![Aqaba–Amman National Water Carrier](https://jordantimes.com/jordantimes/uploads/images/2026/07/05/752890.webp)

### Financing and technical structure

A World Bank Group Guarantees/MIGA project summary records an application for up to **US$1.25 billion** in political-risk coverage, approved on **18 December 2025**, for investments associated with the project.

The listed configuration includes:

- Four parallel reverse-osmosis production lines
- Net output of **300 million cubic metres annually**
- A **438-kilometre underground pipeline**
- Three tanks and four pumping stations
- A solar facility of approximately **281 MWp**
- Renewable power intended to supply about **28% of project electricity needs**
- Risk coverage for as long as 20 years
- Environmental classification as **Category A**

Source:

- [MIGA: Aqaba–Amman Water Desalination and Conveyance](https://www.miga.org/project/aqaba-amman-water-desalination-and-conveyance)

### International financial support

The Netherlands pledged a **€31 million grant**, provided through Invest International and managed by the European Investment Bank’s Water Sector Fund.

Source:

- [Dutch financial support for the Aqaba–Amman project](https://www.dutchwatersector.com/news/major-desalination-project-in-jordan-receives-a-financial-boost-from-the-netherlands)

![Aqaba waterfront and desalination project area](https://www.dutchwatersector.com/sites/default/files/styles/article_x2/public/2025-05/Scenic%20Waterfront%20View%20of%20Aqaba%2C%20Jordan-pexels-dmdwiri99-30338734.jpg?h=2848f5af&itok=EvL2rS3t)

### Strategic and humanitarian significance

Jordan’s reported water deficit is approximately **400 million cubic metres**, while earlier government reporting said the project could provide a sustainable supply for about **four million people**. The desalinated water should also reduce pressure on depleted groundwater and surface-water resources.

This makes the project relevant not only to household water access but also to:

- Public health and humanitarian resilience
- Agricultural and food-system stability
- Climate adaptation
- Reduced dependence on overdrawn aquifers and external supplies
- Renewable-energy integration into critical infrastructure

Earlier schedules anticipated construction in 2025, while another report mentioned 2029. The **July 2026 update—construction in Q4 2026 and pumping in Q4 2030—is the newest and therefore the most authoritative schedule among the retrieved reports**.

Additional sources:

- [Earlier government project timetable and expected coverage](https://en.royanews.tv/news/57133/Jordan%E2%80%99s-largest-water-infrastructure-project-set-to-begin-in-2025)
- [Water deficit and earlier timetable reporting](https://www.zawya.com/en/projects/utilities/construction-on-national-water-carrier-project-in-jordan-to-commence-in-2029-minister-lidvxs8u)
- [Background on Jordan’s water crisis and desalination strategy](https://www.thenationalnews.com/mena/2023/04/30/jordan-plans-multi-billion-dollar-aqaba-to-amman-desalination-project-amid-water-crisis/)

---

## 4. UAE relevance by requested sector

| Sector | Latest evidence found | UAE connection identified |
|---|---|---|
| Diplomacy and security | Jordan condemns attacks on Bahrain, Qatar and Kuwait and calls for a comprehensive approach to Iran and its proxies | **Indirect but significant:** supports GCC security; no new bilateral UAE–Jordan initiative was identified |
| Investment | Germany’s €684 million package and international financing for the water carrier | No UAE investor or financing commitment named |
| Trade | Jordan and Venezuela are developing a roadmap covering trade and investment | No recent UAE–Jordan trade agreement identified |
| Food security | Water carrier should strengthen water availability and reduce pressure on depleted resources | Strong thematic relevance, but no UAE food-security partnership named |
| AI | No relevant announcement retrieved | No UAE–Jordan AI initiative identified |
| Humanitarian affairs | Water infrastructure is expected to improve reliable access for millions | No new UAE humanitarian contribution identified |
| Infrastructure | National Water Carrier moving toward Q4 2026 construction | No UAE contractor, investor or sovereign fund named |
| Energy | Solar plant planned alongside desalination; Jordan discussing oil and gas with Venezuela | No direct UAE energy role identified |

## Overall assessment

Jordan’s immediate policy focus is **regional security, economic reform and water resilience**. For UAE stakeholders, the strongest areas to monitor are the National Water Carrier’s financing and contracting, renewable power for desalination, water-linked food security, and coordination with Jordan on Iranian threats to Gulf states. At present, however, the available reports do **not** document a fresh, direct UAE commitment in those areas.

xsearch.answer:
I couldn’t verify any qualifying live X posts, so I can’t provide a reliable “latest posts” list without inventing URLs or engagement figures.

The search attempted on **July 18, 2026 (approximately 00:00 UTC)** returned no usable results because:

- Direct browsing of X/Twitter URLs was unavailable.
- No dedicated X search access was available to retrieve canonical posts.
- Web searches using `site:x.com` did not surface relevant, verifiable posts.
- Consequently, post text, author affiliation, publication date, verification status, and engagement counts could not be confirmed.

This result is based on the failed live-search attempt rather than real-time X data. No candidate posts, duplicates, or reposts were identified.

hero.leadership: Ayman Safadi serves as Jordanian Foreign Minister and Deputy Prime Minister; the material does not identify Jordan's head of state or head of government.
hero.latestDevelopment: The Aqaba–Amman National Water Carrier is expected to begin construction in Q4 2026, with water pumping scheduled for Q4 2030. It is designed to produce 300 million cubic metres of drinking water annually, approximately 40% of Jordan's drinking-water needs.

items.headline: Aqaba–Amman National Water Carrier advances toward Q4 2026 construction
items.summary: Jordan is preparing final agreements for a major desalination, transmission and renewable-energy project expected to supply 300 million cubic metres of drinking water annually.
items.whatHappened: A 5 July 2026 update said construction is expected to begin in Q4 2026 and take approximately four years, with pumping scheduled for Q4 2030. The project includes a Red Sea desalination plant, roughly 450 kilometres of transmission infrastructure and a solar-power facility.
items.whyImportant: The project is expected to meet approximately 40% of Jordan's drinking-water needs, support about four million people and reduce pressure on depleted groundwater and surface-water resources.
items.whyNow: Jordan has a reported annual water deficit of approximately 400 million cubic metres. Final agreements needed for financial close were still being prepared ahead of the planned construction start.

uaeImpact.reasoning: The project aligns with UAE interests in infrastructure, renewable energy, climate resilience and water-linked food security, but no UAE investor, contractor, sovereign fund or bilateral commitment was identified.

items.aidRequired: No specific aid request was reported. Remaining needs include completion of financial-close agreements and mobilisation of project financing.
items.investmentPotential: Potential exists across desalination, a 438–450 kilometre transmission system, pumping stations, storage tanks and an approximately 281 MWp solar facility. No UAE participation has been reported.
recommendedActions: Monitor financial-close and procurement announcements for the Q4 2026 construction schedule.
Assess renewable-power, desalination and infrastructure participation without assuming an existing UAE role.
Evaluate water-security and food-security implications for future UAE–Jordan cooperation.

items.headline: Jordan takes a firmer position on Iran and attacks against Gulf states
items.summary: Ayman Safadi called for a comprehensive agreement addressing Iranian proxy support and destabilising activity, while Jordan condemned reported Iranian attacks against Bahrain, Qatar and Kuwait.
items.whatHappened: Jordan's Foreign Ministry described the reported attacks against Bahrain, Qatar and Kuwait as heinous. Safadi separately argued that any agreement with Iran should address proxy groups and broader destabilising activities.
items.whyImportant: The statements indicate Jordanian alignment with Gulf security concerns and opposition to attacks on GCC countries and Iranian proxy activity.
items.whyNow: The position was expressed amid reported attacks on Gulf states and diplomatic discussion of a comprehensive approach to Iran.

uaeImpact.reasoning: The UAE was not named in the reported condemnation, but Jordan's position directly supports the broader security of GCC states and creates scope for diplomatic and defence-policy coordination.

items.aidRequired: No humanitarian or financial aid requirement was specified.
items.investmentPotential: No investment opportunity was identified in the reporting.
recommendedActions: Maintain diplomatic coordination with Jordan on Iranian threats and proxy activity affecting Gulf security.
Monitor whether Jordan's statements develop into formal regional-security initiatives.
Avoid treating the reported statements as a new bilateral UAE–Jordan agreement.

items.headline: Germany allocates €684 million to Jordan's reform and development priorities
items.summary: Jordan and Germany signed official minutes allocating €684 million to projects aligned with economic reform, public-sector reform and wider development priorities.
items.whatHappened: Jordanian Planning and International Cooperation Minister Zeina Toukan and Germany's Federal Ministry for Economic Cooperation and Development concluded bilateral development-cooperation talks and signed the official minutes.
items.whyImportant: The package demonstrates the scale of external financing being mobilised for Jordan's reform and development programme.
items.whyNow: Jordan is pursuing economic and public-sector reforms while seeking substantial international development financing.

uaeImpact.reasoning: No UAE participation was reported, but the package may help identify Jordanian reform priorities and areas where UAE investment or development engagement could complement existing international financing.

items.aidRequired: The €684 million package addresses Jordan's stated reform and development priorities; no additional quantified requirement was reported.
items.investmentPotential: Potential may emerge from projects implemented under the economic-reform and development programme, although no specific investable project or UAE role was named.
recommendedActions: Track the projects selected under the €684 million package.
Identify areas where UAE investment could complement rather than duplicate German development financing.
Request project-level information before assigning capital or partners.

items.headline: Jordan and Venezuela agree to develop an economic-cooperation roadmap
items.summary: Jordan and Venezuela plan cooperation covering energy, oil and gas, tourism, culture, trade, investment and political consultations.
items.whatHappened: During a working visit to Caracas, Ayman Safadi met Acting President Delcy Rodríguez and Foreign Minister Yvan Gil. The parties agreed to develop a roadmap and accelerate bilateral agreements.
items.whyImportant: The initiative indicates Jordan's effort to diversify diplomatic and economic relationships, including in hydrocarbons, trade and investment.
items.whyNow: The roadmap follows high-level meetings in Caracas and reflects an effort to translate political engagement into sector-specific cooperation.

uaeImpact.reasoning: No UAE role or direct effect was reported. The development is relevant mainly for monitoring Jordan's diversification of energy, trade and investment relationships.

items.investmentPotential: Potential cooperation was identified in energy, oil and gas, tourism, culture, trade and investment, but no project values or contracts were disclosed.
recommendedActions: Monitor whether the roadmap results in signed energy, trade or investment agreements.
Assess any future implications for Jordan's energy sourcing and commercial relationships.
Do not infer UAE participation from the announced Jordan–Venezuela roadmap.

analysis.executiveSummary: Jordan's principal strategic development is the Aqaba–Amman National Water Carrier, designed to produce 300 million cubic metres of drinking water annually and meet approximately 40% of national drinking-water needs. Construction is expected in Q4 2026, but financial-close agreements were still being prepared, creating both a major infrastructure opportunity and execution risk. Jordan is also adopting a firm security posture toward Iran and attacks on Gulf states, mobilising €684 million in German development financing and diversifying economic relations through a proposed roadmap with Venezuela. No new direct UAE–Jordan investment, trade, AI, energy, infrastructure or humanitarian agreement was identified. UAE relevance is therefore strongest in potential water, renewable-energy and food-security engagement and in diplomatic coordination on Gulf security. Coverage remains incomplete because live-search and X retrieval attempts failed to produce comprehensive, verifiable UAE-specific results.
